content.text: Security responsibilities in Azure are divided between Microsoft and the customer according to the defense-in-depth model. Microsoft secures the outermost layers (physical datacenter, network backbone, hypervisor), while the customer is responsible for configuring inner layers: virtual network controls, OS hardening, application security, identity governance, and data protection. The boundary shifts inward as the service model moves from IaaS to PaaS to SaaS, but identity and data protection remain permanently with the customer.

attrs.alt: Defense-in-depth security layers with customer vs Microsoft ownership

content.text: Microsoft's Security Responsibilities

Microsoft's security obligations cover the physical and infrastructure layers of Azure. These are implemented once at the platform level and benefit all tenants. Microsoft's security practices are verified by independent auditors and documented through compliance certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, PCI DSS Level 1, FedRAMP High) and are published in the Microsoft Trust Center.
Physical datacenter security:

badge access with multi-factor authentication (PIN + biometric), 24/7 security personnel and surveillance, mantrap entry controls, physical cage isolation per tenant where applicable, and background checks for all personnel with datacenter access.
Hardware supply chain security:

Microsoft controls its hardware supply chain, uses Trusted Platform Module (TPM) attestation to verify host integrity at boot, and has a formal process for secure decommissioning and physical destruction of storage media.
Hypervisor isolation:

the Azure hypervisor enforces memory isolation between tenant VMs. A CVE in a guest OS does not allow an attacker to escape to the hypervisor or read another tenant's memory. Microsoft patches the hypervisor and host OS transparently.
Network perimeter DDoS:

Azure DDoS Basic protection absorbs volumetric attacks against the Azure network fabric at no additional cost. DDoS Standard (customer-purchased) adds adaptive tuning and telemetry for specific public IPs.
Global network encryption:
Microsoft encrypts all traffic traversing the Azure backbone between datacenters, using MACsec (IEEE 802.1AE) at the physical layer on inter-datacenter links.
Identity Security: Always the Customer

Identity is the new security perimeter in cloud computing. Network perimeters are porous by design in cloud environments — services expose HTTPS endpoints that are accessible over the internet. Correctly configured identity controls (MFA, Conditional Access, PIM, managed identities) prevent attackers who phish or brute-force credentials from accessing Azure resources. Microsoft provides the identity platform (Entra ID) and the tools (Conditional Access, PIM, Identity Protection), but the customer must configure and enforce them.
Identity control
Why customer must configure it
Consequence of not configuring
Multi-factor authentication
Default user accounts in Entra ID do not require MFA
Phished password = full account compromise
Conditional Access policies
MFA is insufficient without risk-based access controls
Attacker from anonymous IP can authenticate if they have credentials
Privileged Identity Management
Admin roles are permanent by default
Compromised admin account has permanent global admin access
Service principal key rotation
Client secrets and certificates have expiry dates that must be managed
Expired credentials break apps; leaked keys have infinite validity until rotated
Managed identities over service principals
Service principals use secrets/certs that can be leaked; managed identities have no extractable credential
Application config files containing client secrets are common credential leaks
RBAC least privilege
Default RBAC assignments grant broad access (Contributor = full write access)
Over-privileged accounts expand blast radius of any compromise

# Identity security is always the customer's responsibility

# 1. Require MFA for admin roles via Conditional Access
# (Configured in Entra ID portal → Security → Conditional Access)
# Policy: require MFA for users in 'Global Admin' or 'Security Admin' role

# 2. Enable Entra ID Identity Protection (risk-based sign-in policies)
# Risk policies require Entra ID P2 license
# Configured in Entra ID portal → Security → Identity Protection

# 3. Enable Privileged Identity Management (PIM) for eligible role assignments
# PIM makes admin access just-in-time; users must activate before using admin roles
# Configured in Entra ID portal → Identity Governance → Privileged Identity Management

# 4. Audit privileged role assignments (who has Owner/Contributor/etc.)
az role assignment list \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId> \
  --query "[?roleDefinitionName=='Owner' || roleDefinitionName=='Contributor' || roleDefinitionName=='User Access Administrator'].{principal:principalName, role:roleDefinitionName}" \
  --include-inherited \
  --output table

# 5. Audit service principals with elevated access
az role assignment list \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId> \
  --query "[?principalType=='ServicePrincipal'].{principal:principalName, role:roleDefinitionName, scope:scope}" \
  --output table

# 6. Rotate storage account access keys (customer responsibility — keys don't expire)
az storage account keys renew \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --account-name myStorage \
  --key primary
Network Security Controls

Virtual network security is the customer's responsibility in IaaS and partially shared in PaaS. Microsoft provides the tools (NSG, Azure Firewall, DDoS Standard, Private Link) but does not apply them by default. New VMs are not automatically placed behind a firewall; new App Service instances are publicly accessible by default. The customer must design and configure the network security architecture.

# Network security: customer's responsibility in IaaS; shared config in PaaS

# Enable Azure DDoS Network Protection on a VNet (upgrades from Basic to Standard)
az network ddos-protection create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myDDoSPlan \
  --location eastus

az network vnet update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVNet \
  --ddos-protection true \
  --ddos-protection-plan myDDoSPlan

# Deploy Azure Firewall in a hub VNet (centralized network security)
az network firewall create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myFirewall \
  --location eastus \
  --vnet-name myHubVNet \
  --public-ip-addresses myFirewallPIP \
  --sku-tier Standard

# Deny all outbound internet from spoke VNets (route through firewall)
az network route-table create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name spoke-rt

az network route-table route create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --route-table-name spoke-rt \
  --name default-to-firewall \
  --next-hop-type VirtualAppliance \
  --next-hop-ip-address $(az network firewall ip-config list -g myRG -f myFirewall --query '[0].privateIpAddress' -o tsv) \
  --address-prefix 0.0.0.0/0

# Enable Azure Firewall Threat Intelligence (block known malicious IPs/domains)
az network firewall policy update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myFirewallPolicy \
  --threat-intel-mode Deny
Data Security: Encryption and Classification

Data security encompasses encryption at rest, encryption in transit, classification, and lifecycle management. Microsoft encrypts all data at rest in Azure Storage, SQL, and other services using AES-256 with platform-managed keys (PMK). This satisfies the provider's encryption obligation. The customer must decide whether PMK is sufficient or whether customer-managed keys (CMK) in Key Vault are required (common for regulated industries). The customer is also responsible for classifying data (what is sensitive, what is public, what is regulated), applying sensitivity labels, configuring DLP policies, and managing data retention.

# Data security: always customer responsibility regardless of service model

# Enable customer-managed key (CMK) for Azure Storage
# 1. Create a Key Vault with soft-delete and purge protection (required for CMK)
az keyvault create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myCMKVault \
  --location eastus \
  --enable-soft-delete true \
  --enable-purge-protection true

# 2. Create a key in Key Vault
az keyvault key create \
  --vault-name myCMKVault \
  --name storageEncryptionKey \
  --kty RSA \
  --size 2048

# 3. Grant storage account's managed identity access to the key
STORAGE_IDENTITY=$(az storage account show -g myRG -n myStorage --query identity.principalId -o tsv)
az keyvault set-policy \
  --name myCMKVault \
  --object-id $STORAGE_IDENTITY \
  --key-permissions get wrapKey unwrapKey

# 4. Enable CMK on the storage account
az storage account update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myStorage \
  --encryption-key-source Microsoft.Keyvault \
  --encryption-key-vault https://myCMKVault.vault.azure.net \
  --encryption-key-name storageEncryptionKey \
  --encryption-key-version ''

# Enable double encryption (AES-256 on top of AES-256)
az storage account update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myStorage \
  --require-infrastructure-encryption true
Application Security

Application code, frameworks, and dependencies are always the customer's responsibility regardless of service model. Microsoft does not inspect or scan customer application code running on App Service, Azure Functions, or VMs. Common application security failures in Azure include: SQL injection vulnerabilities in customer code, secrets (connection strings, API keys) stored in environment variables or source control, outdated framework versions with known CVEs, and missing input validation on HTTP endpoints.

# Application security is always the customer's responsibility

# Enable Defender for App Service (detect web attacks, anomalous behavior)
az security pricing create \
  --name AppServices \
  --tier Standard

# Enable Application Gateway WAF v2 in prevention mode
az network application-gateway waf-config set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --gateway-name myAppGW \
  --enabled true \
  --firewall-mode Prevention \
  --rule-set-type OWASP \
  --rule-set-version 3.2

# Scan container images for vulnerabilities before deployment
az acr check-health \
  --name myACR \
  --yes

# Enable Defender for Containers to scan images pushed to ACR
az security pricing create \
  --name Containers \
  --tier Standard

# App Service: restrict source IP for deployment slots (staging)
az webapp config access-restriction add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWebApp \
  --slot staging \
  --rule-name 'OfficeOnly' \
  --action Allow \
  --ip-address 203.0.113.0/24 \
  --priority 100

# Disable remote debugging (must be customer's explicit action)
az webapp config set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWebApp \
  --remote-debugging-enabled false
Security Monitoring and Incident Response

Detection and response capabilities are the customer's responsibility. Microsoft Defender for Cloud, Microsoft Sentinel (SIEM), and Azure Monitor provide the infrastructure for detection — but the customer must enable the services, configure alert rules, connect data sources, and staff an incident response process. A Defender for Cloud alert for a brute-force SSH attempt against a VM does not automatically block the attacker; it requires the customer to triage the alert and respond.

# Security monitoring: customer must configure (not automatic)

# Enable Microsoft Sentinel (SIEM) on a Log Analytics workspace
az security insights create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --workspace-name myWorkspace

# Connect Azure Activity Logs to Sentinel (detect control-plane changes)
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --resource /subscriptions/<subId> \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Subscription \
  --name sub-activity-logs \
  --workspace $(az monitor log-analytics workspace show -g myRG -n myWorkspace --query id -o tsv) \
  --logs '[{"category":"Administrative","enabled":true},{"category":"Security","enabled":true},{"category":"Alert","enabled":true}]'

# Create an alert for suspicious sign-ins (failed MFA attempts)
az monitor scheduled-query create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name FailedMFAAlert \
  --scopes $(az monitor log-analytics workspace show -g myRG -n myWorkspace --query id -o tsv) \
  --condition-query 'SigninLogs | where ResultType == "50074" or ResultType == "50076" | summarize count() by UserPrincipalName, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m) | where count_ > 5' \
  --condition-time-aggregation Count \
  --condition-operator GreaterThan \
  --condition-threshold 0 \
  --evaluation-frequency PT5M \
  --window-size PT15M \
  --severity 2 \
  --action-groups $(az monitor action-group show -g myRG -n myActionGroup --query id -o tsv)

# View Defender for Cloud alerts (customer must triage these)
az security alert list \
  --query "[].{alertType:properties.alertType, severity:properties.severity, time:properties.timeGeneratedUtc, resource:properties.compromisedEntity}" \
  --output table

The shared responsibility model creates a clear accountability boundary: Microsoft is responsible for securing the platform, and customers are responsible for securing their use of the platform. The platform has never been breached at scale; the incidents that make headlines are customer misconfigurations, phished credentials, and vulnerable application code.
Patch Management (IaaS Customer Responsibility)

For IaaS virtual machines, OS patching is the customer's responsibility. Azure provides Azure Update Manager as the recommended tool for assessing patch compliance, scheduling patch deployments within maintenance windows, and tracking patch history across a fleet of VMs. The customer must decide the patching cadence, configure maintenance windows to minimize downtime, and verify that critical and security patches are applied in a timely manner. Microsoft's SLA does not require OS patches to be applied — the customer is solely responsible for the risk of running unpatched VMs.

# Patch management: customer responsibility for IaaS guest OS

# Enable Azure Update Manager (successor to Update Management in Automation)
# Assesses VMs for missing patches and can schedule installation

# Check patch assessment for all VMs in a resource group
az vm assess-patches \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM

# Install all critical and security patches immediately
az vm install-patches \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --maximum-duration PT1H \
  --reboot-setting IfRequired \
  --classifications Critical Security

# Configure a scheduled patch deployment (maintenance window)
az maintenance configuration create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name weeklyPatchWindow \
  --maintenance-scope InGuestPatch \
  --location eastus \
  --recur-every Week \
  --duration 02:00 \
  --start-date-time '2026-07-01 02:00' \
  --time-zone 'UTC' \
  --in-guest-patch-mode User \
  --linux-classifications-to-include Critical Security Other

# Assign the maintenance configuration to a VM
az maintenance assignment create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --resource-name myVM \
  --resource-type virtualMachines \
  --provider-name Microsoft.Compute \
  --configuration-assignment-name weeklyPatchAssignment \
  --maintenance-configuration-id \
  $(az maintenance configuration show -g myRG -n weeklyPatchWindow --query id -o tsv)
Patch mode options for VMs:

AutomaticByPlatform (Microsoft applies patches during maintenance windows), AutomaticByOS (guest OS applies patches automatically via Windows Update/yum), and Manual (customer applies all patches manually). AutomaticByPlatform is recommended for production VMs.
Hotpatch (Windows Server 2022):
allows monthly security patches to be applied without rebooting the VM. Customer must opt in to Azure Automanage and select a hotpatch-capable VM image.
Linux distributions:

Azure Marketplace images for Ubuntu, RHEL, and SUSE are maintained by Canonical, Red Hat, and SUSE respectively — the customer still decides when to apply patches and must configure the update agent (unattended-upgrades for Ubuntu, dnf-automatic for RHEL).
Key Management and Azure Key Vault

Encryption key management is one of the most consequential customer security responsibilities. When using platform-managed keys (PMK), Microsoft holds the keys and encrypts data transparently. When using customer-managed keys (CMK), the customer stores keys in Azure Key Vault and Microsoft cannot decrypt customer data without access to the key. For regulated workloads (PCI DSS, HIPAA, classified government), CMK is often required. Key Vault provides soft delete (90-day recovery window), purge protection (prevents permanent deletion), and access logging. Customer responsibilities include: enabling purge protection, rotating keys before expiry, monitoring for failed access attempts, and restricting Key Vault access to private endpoints.

# Key management: customer controls encryption keys — Microsoft cannot access CMK

# Create Key Vault with all protection features enabled
az keyvault create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mySecureVault \
  --location eastus \
  --sku premium \
  --enable-soft-delete true \
  --soft-delete-retention-days 90 \
  --enable-purge-protection true \
  --enable-rbac-authorization true

# Disable public access to Key Vault (force Private Endpoint only)
az keyvault update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mySecureVault \
  --public-network-access Disabled

# Create Key Vault private endpoint
az network private-endpoint create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name kvPrivateEndpoint \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet AppSubnet \
  --private-connection-resource-id $(az keyvault show -g myRG -n mySecureVault --query id -o tsv) \
  --group-id vault \
  --connection-name kv-connection

# Rotate a secret programmatically (Key Vault Event Grid + Function)
# Set rotation policy on a secret (auto-rotate before expiry)
az keyvault secret set-attributes \
  --vault-name mySecureVault \
  --name myConnectionString \
  --expires '2026-12-31T23:59:59Z'

# Alert on secrets expiring within 30 days
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name kvSecretExpiryAlert \
  --scopes $(az keyvault show -g myRG -n mySecureVault --query id -o tsv) \
  --condition category=Administrative and operationName=Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/secrets/expiry/approaching \
  --action-group $(az monitor action-group show -g myRG -n myActionGroup --query id -o tsv)
Threat Detection and Just-in-Time Access

Defender for Cloud (formerly Azure Security Center) is the primary threat detection service for Azure workloads, but it must be enabled and configured by the customer. In its free tier, it provides basic security posture recommendations. In its Standard/P2 tier (Defender plans), it provides threat detection: brute-force SSH/RDP alerts, malware detection on VMs, anomalous SQL queries, suspicious storage access, and container runtime threats. Just-in-time (JIT) VM access replaces always-open management ports (RDP 3389, SSH 22) with on-demand access — the customer requests access, Defender for Cloud creates a temporary NSG rule for the specified source IP and duration, then automatically revokes it. This eliminates the attack surface of persistently open management ports.

# Threat detection: customer must enable and configure Defender plans

# Enable Defender for Cloud (all resource types)
for PLAN in VirtualMachines SqlServers AppServices StorageAccounts KeyVaults Arm Dns Containers; do
  az security pricing create --name $PLAN --tier Standard
done

# Enable just-in-time (JIT) VM access on a VM
# (replaces always-open RDP/SSH with on-demand access)
az security jit-policy create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --location eastus \
  --virtual-machines '[{"id":"/subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM","ports":[{"number":22,"protocol":"TCP","allowedSourceAddressPrefix":"*","maxRequestAccessDuration":"PT3H"},{"number":3389,"protocol":"TCP","allowedSourceAddressPrefix":"*","maxRequestAccessDuration":"PT3H"}]}]'

# Request JIT access to a VM (NSG rule is created temporarily for 3 hours)
az security jit-policy initiate \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --location eastus \
  --vm-id /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM \
  --ports '[{"number":22,"duration":"PT1H","allowedSourceAddressPrefix":"203.0.113.1"}]'

# View Defender security alerts (customer must triage)
az security alert list \
  --query "[?properties.severity=='High'].{type:properties.alertType, time:properties.timeGeneratedUtc, entity:properties.compromisedEntity, desc:properties.description}" \
  --output table
Security control
Microsoft provides
Customer must
OS patching (IaaS)
Azure Update Manager tool
Configure patch mode, maintenance window, apply patches
Endpoint detection (IaaS)
Microsoft Defender for Endpoint
Enable Defender for Servers plan, deploy MDE extension
Encryption at rest
AES-256 with PMK by default
Configure CMK in Key Vault if required; enable purge protection
Network threat detection
Azure DDoS Basic
Enable DDoS Standard; configure Azure Firewall threat intelligence
Identity threat detection
Entra ID sign-in risk scoring
Enable Identity Protection; configure risk-based Conditional Access
SIEM
Microsoft Sentinel platform
Enable workspace, connect data sources, configure analytics rules
Vulnerability assessment
Defender for Cloud integration (Qualys/Defender EASM)
Enable Defender for Servers; remediate findings
Secret management
Azure Key Vault platform
Enable soft delete + purge protection; configure rotation; use Private Endpoint
Incident Response Runbook

Incident response is the customer's operational responsibility. Microsoft does not investigate security incidents on behalf of customers, remediate compromised workloads, or contain breaches — except in cases where Azure platform infrastructure is compromised (an extremely rare event covered by Microsoft's own incident response process). For customer workloads, the customer must have a documented incident response plan: how to detect an incident (SIEM alerts, Defender for Cloud notifications), how to contain it (isolate the compromised resource, revoke credentials), how to investigate (preserve evidence, review audit logs), and how to recover (restore from backup, harden before returning to production).
Detect:
Microsoft Defender for Cloud and Microsoft Sentinel provide alerts. The customer must configure alert routing to the security operations team (email, PagerDuty, Teams channel via Action Groups).
Contain:
isolate a compromised VM by removing NSG rules or blocking its NIC; disable a compromised user account in Entra ID; revoke active tokens; rotate compromised credentials.
Investigate:

preserve disk snapshots before wiping; review Azure Activity Log for all control-plane actions during the incident window; review Entra ID sign-in logs and audit logs; review application logs in Log Analytics.
Eradicate and recover:
redeploy from a known-clean image (immutable infrastructure); restore data from backup taken before compromise; apply missed patches; harden the configuration that was exploited.
Post-incident:
document the root cause (which customer-responsibility control was missing or misconfigured); update runbooks; consider whether to engage Microsoft Support for guidance.

# Incident response: customer must have playbooks and runbooks

# Step 1: Identify the compromised resource
az security alert list \
  --query "[?properties.severity=='High'].{alert:properties.alertType, resource:properties.compromisedEntity, time:properties.timeGeneratedUtc}" \
  --output table

# Step 2: Isolate compromised VM (remove from NSG — deny all inbound)
az network nsg rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --nsg-name myNSG \
  --name ISOLATE-EMERGENCY \
  --priority 100 \
  --source-address-prefixes '*' \
  --destination-port-ranges '*' \
  --access Deny \
  --direction Inbound

# Step 3: Capture disk snapshot for forensic analysis
DISK_ID=$(az vm show -g myRG -n myVM --query 'storageProfile.osDisk.managedDisk.id' -o tsv)
az snapshot create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name forensic-snapshot-$(date +%Y%m%d) \
  --source $DISK_ID \
  --location eastus

# Step 4: Revoke active sessions for compromised identity
# (Microsoft Graph API or Entra ID portal)
# az rest --method POST --uri 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/<userId>/revokeSignInSessions'

# Step 5: Reset compromised account password and regenerate credentials
az ad user update \
  --id compromised@contoso.com \
  --account-enabled false

# Step 6: Review Activity Log for all actions taken during incident window
az monitor activity-log list \
  --caller 'compromised@contoso.com' \
  --start-time '2026-06-29T00:00:00Z' \
  --end-time '2026-06-30T23:59:59Z' \
  --query "[].{operation:operationName.localizedValue, time:eventTimestamp, resource:resourceId}" \
  --output table

Microsoft Support can help with Azure platform issues during an incident (is the Azure service itself experiencing an outage? is a platform feature broken?) but does not perform forensic investigation, contain customer workload compromises, or provide incident response services. Those are the customer's operational responsibility, typically supported by a dedicated SOC team or contracted MSSP.
Summary

Security responsibilities in Azure follow a defense-in-depth model. Microsoft is permanently responsible for physical datacenter security, hardware integrity, hypervisor isolation, the global network fabric, and platform DDoS protection. The customer is permanently responsible for identity and access management (MFA, Conditional Access, PIM, service principal governance), application security (code quality, dependency management, secrets management), and data security (encryption key management, classification, DLP, retention). In IaaS, the customer also owns OS patching, guest OS hardening, host-based security tools, and virtual network security. In PaaS, OS and runtime patching transfers to Microsoft, but the customer must still configure network access controls (firewall rules, private endpoints) and authentication settings. In SaaS, Microsoft manages the application, but identity governance and data protection remain with the customer. Detection and response (SIEM, alerts, incident response) are the customer's operational obligation across all models.
